content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 27, 2017

Michael Torres
Assistant City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
City of Newport Beach
100 Civic Center Drive
Newport Beach, CA 92660

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-193

This letter responds to your request for advice, on behalf of Councilmember Diane Dixon, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the Act).¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May Councilmember Dixon take part in decisions involving the application and development of the Koll Center Project located near the Pacific Club, where she is a corporate re-admit member?

CONCLUSION

Yes. There will be no reasonably foreseeable financial effects on any of Councilmember Dixon's interests.

FACTS

The facts you provide state that Councilmember Dixon was elected to the Newport Beach City Council on November 4, 2014. She is a member of The Pacific Club, a private social and fitness club in Newport Beach, California.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-193
Page No. 2

The Pacific Club is a 501(c)(7) tax exempt organization located at 4110 MacArthur
Boulevard (the Club). The Club has an approximately 36,000 square foot building. It provides its
members facilities for breakfast, lunch, and dinner, private meeting rooms, conference rooms, bars,
lounging areas, and a full amenity spa and fitness center. The Club offers four primary classes of
membership: regular, junior, sustaining and non-residential. It also offers a corporate re-admit
membership, which allows an individual who previously had an employer's corporate membership
to join as a personal member once separated from the employer.

Councilmember Dixon is a corporate re-admit member, having obtained her membership
while employed with a local corporation. Corporate re-admit members pay monthly dues of
approximately $495.00, but are not required to pay an initiation fee. A corporate re-admit member
does not have the same proprietary benefits as a member under the four primary classes. For
example, corporate re-admit memberships do not provide an ownership interest in the assets of the
Club, they are not transferrable, and they do not provide the power to vote on the board of directors
or for any other matters brought to the vote of the memberships.

Shopoff Land Fund II, L.P. submitted a development application for property adjacent to the
Club called the Koll Center Project (the Project). The Project is a residential development proposed
for 4400 Von Karman Avenue in Newport Beach, which currently houses parking lots and related
commercial development. The Project is composed of three 13-story condominium structures and a
parking garage on roughly 12.5 acres. The Project is visible from the Club; they are separated by
roughly 465 feet across Von Karman Avenue. The Project's development plans are tentatively
scheduled to come before the City Council in late 2017 or early 2018.

Since her election, Councilmember Dixon has recused herself from participating on any
items related to the Project, because of her concern that the Project may have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on her membership in the Club.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.² A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official's interests as set forth in Section 87103.

■ A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

² When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including city attorneys) has a conflict
of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item,
orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest as discussed in Regulation
18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion and/or vote on the item.

File No. A-17-193
Page No. 3

▪ Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.)

▪ A source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

▪ A source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $470 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

▪ The official's personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family --
this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

I. Real Property Interest.

We first address whether Councilmember Dixon's interest in the Club constitutes real
property. In making this determination, we have considered prior letters that address similar
interests in club memberships.

Section 82033 defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold, beneficial or
ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction
owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her
immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.
Interests in real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of
any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."

A. No interest in the Club's assets.

In the Doering Advice Letter, No. A-12-068, we found that a proprietary membership in a
club, a nonprofit corporation registered as a 501(c)(7) nonprofit mutual benefit organization, was
real property. We said that the proprietary members had an equitable right of ownership to the
club's real and personal property because they were entitled to a proportionate share of the value of
the assets if the club were to be dissolved. Doering also said ". . . it is clear that, particularly when
the resale value of a club membership is determined at least in part by the value of the club's real
estate, the members have at least a beneficial interest in that real estate."

In this case, you state that Councilmember Dixon's corporate re-admit membership provides
no ownership interest in the assets of the Club. Consequently, unlike the proprietary club
membership in Doering, her membership does not constitute a real property interest.

B. Value of membership does not affect the Club's assets or value of the land.

A club membership does not constitute an interest in real property where the club holds title
to the land and the value of the membership is not related to increases or decreases in the assets of
the club or the value of the land upon which it is located. (Cook Advice Letter, No. I-91-468;
Greenwell Advice Letter, No. A-97-543.)

File No. A-17-193
Page No. 4

You provide facts stating that Councilmember Dixon has no ownership interest in the assets
of the Club, no right to transfer her membership, and no vote on the Club's board of directors.
Under these facts, the value of the membership is not related to an increase or decrease of the
Club's assets, or the value of the land where the Club is located. Consequently, Councilmember
Dixon does not have an interest in the Club's property within the meaning of Section 87103(b).
(Section 82033; Doering Advice Letter, No. A-12-068; Greenwell Advice Letter, No. A-97-543;
Martyn Advice Letter, No. A-97-378; Wiener Advice Letter, No. A-92-149; Cook Advice Letter,
No. I-91-468.)

II. Investment in a Business Entity.

The Commission has determined that club memberships can be an investment in a business
entity. Where the membership is in a "business entity" as defined in the Act, the membership has a
$2,000 value, and the membership may be resold for a profit or loss, we have advised that the
membership should be treated as an investment in a business entity. (Section 82034.)

However, Councilmember Dixon does not have an investment in a business entity because
her membership cannot be transferred, and thus cannot be resold for profit or loss. Also, as a
501(c)(7) non-profit organization, the Club is not a business entity under the Act, which defines
"business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit. (Section 82005.)
Consequently, Councilmember Dixon does not have an interest in the Club as a business entity
within the meaning of Section 87103(a)

III. Personal Financial Interest.

Under Regulation 18703.5, a public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances and those of his or her immediate family. Based upon the facts provided, the only interest
implicated by your question is Councilmember Dixon's interest in her personal finances, and we
must determine if the effect of a decision regarding property within 500 feet of the Club may have a
foreseeable and material effect on the Councilmember's personal finances.

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) For an
interest in the official's personal finances, a governmental decision will have an effect on this
interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Regulation 18702.5(a).)

Decisions to develop the Project may have an effect on the Club and its property, but these
decisions are not likely to affect Councilmember Dixon's personal expenses, income, assets or
liabilities. Because her membership is not an interest in real property, any changes in the value of

File No. A-17-193
Page No. 5

the Club's real property will have no effect on her asset – the corporate re-admit membership. It is unlikely that any effect on the Club from the condominium project is significant enough to foresee a change in the price of the corporate re-admit membership.

Accordingly, Councilmember Dixon is not prohibited from taking part in the decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: John M. Peser Jr.
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JMF:jgl

development of the Koll Center Project located near the Pacific Club, where she is a corporate re­
admit member?

Dixon's interests.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest.2 A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning 
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
one or more of the public official's interests as set forth in Section 87103. 
• 
A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
2 When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including city attorneys) has a conflict 
of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (I) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, 
orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest as discussed in Regulation 
18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the 
discussion and/or vote on the item.

• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.) 
• A source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more within 
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103( c ). )

months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• The official's personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -­
this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section.87103.)

Real Property Interest. 
We first address whether Councilmember Dixon's interest in the Club constitutes real

Section 82033 defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold, beneficial or

In this case, you state that Councilmember Dixon's corporate re-admit membership provides

the club or the value of the land upon which it is located. (Cook Advice Letter, No. I-91-468; 
Greenwell Advice Letter, No. A-97-543.)  
You provide facts stating that Councilmember Dixon has no ownership interest in the assets 
of the Club, no right to transfer her membership, and no vote on the Club's board of directors. 
Under these facts, the value of the membership is not related to an increase or decrease of the 
Club's assets, or the value of the land where the Club is located. Consequently, Councilmember

No. J-91-468.)

Investment in a Business Entity. 
The Commission has determined that club memberships can be an investment in a business

finances and those of his or her immediate family. Based upon the facts provided, the only interest

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a

an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) For an

Accordingly, Councilmember Dixon is not prohibited from taking part in the decisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Dixon take part in decisions involving the application and 
development of the Koll Center Project located near the Pacific Club, where she is a corporate re­
admit member?

CONCLUSION: Yes. There will be no reasonably foreseeable financial effects on any of Councilmember 
Dixon's interests.
